title: Discipleship 10: The Role of Parents in Discipleship, Part 2
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: Discipleship 10 - The Role of Parents in Discipleship - Part 2 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Review: Bible Sufficient and Discipline Not a Substitute for Regeneration
subpoints: The family must be the primary place where culture is created
Deuteronomy 6:4-9: the Word of God must dominate raising children — sitting, walking, lying down, rising up
Two foundation expectations from last session: Bible is sufficient; discipline does not substitute for regeneration
The goal is believing children, not merely well-behaved children

outline.title: Foundation #3: Godly Child-Rearing Is Covenantal
subpoints: 1 Corinthians 7:13-15: children of at least one believing parent are holy (set apart), not unclean
Covenantal sanctification does not equal regeneration but does mean a special relationship with God
The fact that a child is a sinner not yet professing faith is grounds for watchfulness; covenantal sanctification is grounds for confidence
Parents who are faithful to the covenant have every reason to expect their children will be saved
Psalm 103:17-18: the mercy of the Lord is to children's children of those who keep the covenant

outline.title: Foundation #4: The Responsibility for Child-Rearing Belongs to the Father
subpoints: It is absolutely essential for both parents to recognize that the final responsibility rests with the father (Ephesians 6:4)
He cannot pass it to the wife, blame the wife, or be led by the wife — that is headship
Masculine initiative: watching over the family without prompting; seeking information about children at his own instigation
Know what is going on in your house, in your children's education, and what they struggle with
Honoring the mother: children should be as or more responsive to the mother than the father — if not, the father has failed
Child-rearing is not a competition between father and mother; they are on the same team

outline.title: Foundation #5: Young Children Are Not Equipped for Independence
subpoints: Young children need full dependence and authority; grown children should be fully independent
Ted Tripp's chart: in early years, high authority over children but low influence; as they mature, authority decreases and influence increases
If parents do the hard work early, they will be spared much grief later
Many parents reverse this order — failing to do the work early, then trying to control everything later

outline.title: The Covenant Promises and Parental Faithfulness
subpoints: God promises to be a God to us and to our children — a promise throughout Scripture
The norm for faithful covenant members is that their children will follow in their faithfulness
Psalm 102:28; Psalm 103:17-18: transgenerational blessing for those who keep the covenant
This is not automatic transfer of saving grace — it is a gospel promise held out to repentant, faithful parents
Failure to claim these promises often stems from reluctance to accept the converse: that how we raise our children affects their direction
Remedy for parents who have failed: repent, and be a godly father or mother in whatever stage your children are in now
God uses parents as the means of his grace to their children, just as he uses preachers; the use of means does not undermine grace

summary:
This session continues the examination of foundational expectations for Christian parents, picking up with the third of five foundational stones: godly child-rearing is covenantal. Using 1 Corinthians 7:13-15, Pastor Lyro shows that the children of at least one believing parent are 'holy' — set apart in a special covenantal relationship with God. This does not mean automatic salvation, but it does mean that the children of faithful parents are not spiritual orphans: they have been placed under the umbrella of God's promises. This covenantal status is both grounds for confidence (God promises to be a God to our children) and grounds for watchfulness (the child is still a sinner who needs regeneration and must eventually make a personal profession of faith).

The fourth foundation stone — that the final responsibility for child-rearing belongs to the father, not the mother — receives extended treatment. Ephesians 6:4 addresses fathers specifically: bring them up in the training and admonition of the Lord. This requires masculine initiative: knowing what is happening in the family, knowing each child, and never defaulting to 'go ask your mother.' The father must also ensure that children honor the mother; if they are more responsive to dad than to mom, this is a failure of leadership. The fifth foundation is that young children are not equipped for independence and should be given high levels of parental authority early, with that authority gradually transferring to influence as they mature.

The session closes with a substantial treatment of the covenant promises of God to faithful parents. Psalm 102:28 and 103:17-18 promise transgenerational blessing for those who keep the covenant. The conversion experience of the lifelong believer — 'I cannot remember when Jesus was not my Lord' — should be the normal testimony in covenant homes, not the exotic exception. Parents who have failed are called to repent and to be faithful now, in whatever stage their children are. God uses parents as means of grace just as he uses preachers; the existence of means does not undermine sovereign grace.
transcript:
## Review: The Family as Culture

We are continuing in our series on discipleship, looking at how to raise our children in light of God's covenantal promises. We are still in the first of our four areas — the family as the primary place where culture is formed.

By way of review: the biblical family is an instituted government with a constitution (the word of God), a head (the husband), a chief advisor (the wife), and temporary citizens (the children, who are being raised to leave and establish their own families). Every family is its own culture with its own language, customs, and unspoken assumptions. Families that fail to be intentional about forming a godly culture will find that outside influences fill the vacuum.

Fathers must lead in establishing this biblical culture. Mothers must be convinced of its importance. And we laid two foundation stones: the Bible is sufficient for every parenting question, and discipline is no substitute for regeneration.

## Foundation Stone 3: Covenantal Child-Rearing

The third foundational stone is this: **godly child-rearing is covenantal**.

The children of believers, though they share the sinful nature common to all descendants of Adam, have been given a tremendous covenantal privilege. Paul addresses this directly in 1 Corinthians 7:14:

> *"For the unbelieving husband is sanctified by the wife, and the unbelieving wife is sanctified by the husband; otherwise your children would be unclean, but now they are holy."*

The children of believing parents are *holy* — set apart, in a special covenant relationship with God — even before they have made a profession of faith. This does not mean they are automatically saved. But it means they are not ordinary pagans; they are in a special relationship with God through the faith of their parents.

This covenantal status is grounds for *confidence* in raising children. When we look at the promises of Scripture concerning covenant families:

> *"The mercy of the Lord is from everlasting to everlasting on those who fear Him, and His righteousness to children's children, to such as keep His covenant, and to those who remember His commandments to do them."* (Psalm 103:17–18)

Parents who fulfill their covenantal obligations have every reason to expect that their children will be saved. This is not a guarantee of automatic grace transfer. But it is a gospel promise — and promises are different from proverbs. The Bible is full of promises to faithful parents. When we fail to comfort ourselves with these promises, it is often because we are reluctant to face the other side of the same truth: that our faithlessness in raising our children contributes to their turning away.

Proverbs 22:6 — *"Train up a child in the way he should go, and when he is old he will not depart from it"* — is a truism, not a promise. It is a statement of how things generally work. Whatever you win your children with, that is what you win them for.

But the promises are real. Isaiah 65:23 says God's elect *"shall not bring forth children for trouble; for they shall be the descendants of the blessed of the Lord, and their offspring with them."* These are the promises given to covenant-keeping parents.

If you are sitting here knowing that you have failed and that your children are not doing well, and you know your parenting is part of that — what do you do? You repent. You turn to God for forgiveness and walk in his ways from wherever you are now. You cannot undo the past, but you can be faithful from this moment forward.

## Foundation Stone 4: The Father Bears Final Responsibility

The fourth foundation stone is this: **the final responsibility for child-rearing belongs to the father**.

Paul in Ephesians 6:4 addresses the *father* specifically: *"Fathers, do not provoke your children to wrath, but bring them up in the training and admonition of the Lord."* He could have said "parents" — and the previous verse does use that word — but he singles out the father. The burden rests on his shoulders.

This is what headship necessarily involves. The husband leads his wife in child-rearing; he does not react to her or let himself be led by her on this. Leadership requires initiative: it means watching over the family without prompting, seeking information about the children from the wife at your own instigation. Do you know what is going on in your children's education? Do you know what they struggle with, what they need, how to reach them?

There is a temptation for some fathers to take satisfaction in the fact that their children obey them immediately while not minding their mother. That is not biblical leadership — it is failure. Child-rearing is not a competition between father and mother. They are on the same team. When children do not obey their mother as they should, the husband should understand that as a failure on his part and work to correct it. Whenever children look at their mother, they should see the looming shadow of dad behind her, whether he is home or not.

> *"Husbands, likewise, dwell with them with understanding, giving honor to the wife, as to the weaker vessel and as being heirs together of the grace of life, that your prayers may not be hindered."* (1 Peter 3:7)

Leadership in child-rearing also means sacrifice. You cannot be a faithful father without being willing to lay down your life for your children's discipleship. You lead as a shepherd leads — not by sitting in the pen and being waited on, but by going before the flock, providing, guiding, and protecting.

## Foundation Stone 5: Young Children Are Not Ready for Independence

Young children are not equipped for independence. The duty of parents in the early years is to exercise high authority over their children. As Ted Tripp illustrates in *Shepherding a Child's Heart*, in the early years authority is high and influence is limited; as children grow older through faithful parenting, authority gradually decreases while influence — the ability to speak into their lives meaningfully — increases.

Many parents try to reverse this order. They do not do the hard work in the early years and then try to assert control over a teenager. Do the work now, and the influence will follow.

## The Promise to Covenant Parents

One last word: the conversion experience we set before our children matters. We should not be setting before our covenant children the conversion story of someone who had lived far from God and was dramatically rescued. The most glorious testimony for a child of believers is: *"I cannot remember a time when Jesus was not my Lord. I cannot remember when I could not pray to him."* That is the covenantal expectation.

God promises to be a God to us and to our children. For covenantally faithful parents, because the promises of Scripture cannot be broken, the Lord's gracious calling of our children, grandchildren, and great-grandchildren is something we can rest in. Trans-generational blessing is the norm — not the exception — in the covenant community of Christ.
